name: Key And Lock
description: A lock blocks progress until the player presents, consumes, or proves ownership of a required key item.
design_notes: Key and lock puzzles are progression gates with inventory and persistence risks. The design should define key_item_id ownership, lock_id state, consumption policy, duplicate unlock prevention, and whether the gate is optional, required, reusable, or shared across multiple locks.
design_purpose: Gate areas or rewards behind exploration, routing, or item acquisition.
Give players a concrete goal that can be tracked through inventory and level layout.
Support authored progression without requiring complex puzzle logic.
player_fantasy: The player feels they found the right item and used it to open a previously blocked path.

parameters.name: lock_id
parameters.description: Stable lock identifier persisted after the gate is opened or permanently changed.

parameters.name: key_item_id
parameters.description: Required inventory item or quest token checked when the player attempts unlock.

parameters.name: key_consumption_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule that decides whether the key is removed, retained, or marked after successful unlock.

parameters.name: progression_gate_id
parameters.description: Design-facing gate label used by progression validation and route planning.

parameters.name: unlock_transaction_id
parameters.description: Idempotent transaction identifier that prevents duplicate unlocks or double consumption.

edge_cases: The player unlocks a door and reloads before the key removal and lock state are both persisted.
Two players try to unlock the same shared lock with different keys in the same server tick.
A key is optional for one route but required to avoid a later soft-lock.
The lock is opened by script, physics, or quest state before the inventory key is used.
common_bugs: The key is consumed but the lock remains closed after an interrupted animation or scene transition.
Unlock prompts appear for players who lack the key because UI checks a stale inventory snapshot.
Duplicate interaction events consume multiple stack units for one lock.
Save data stores door animation state but not the authoritative lock_id opened flag.
balancing_notes: Required keys should be placed where players can infer the route back to the lock.
Reusable keys reduce inventory friction but can weaken the sense of one-time progression.
Optional locks work well when their rewards justify revisiting without blocking the main path.
accessibility_notes: Show the required key name or icon in text form, and make missing-key messages persistent enough to read.
Consider map or journal reminders for locks the player has found but cannot yet open.

implementation_notes.unity: Use a lock component keyed by lock_id that requests an inventory transaction with key_item_id and unlock_transaction_id. Commit lock state and key consumption together, then drive Animator, audio, and UI from the committed result.
implementation_notes.godot: Keep lock state in a puzzle state resource or autoload store, not only on the door node. Interaction RPC or signal handlers should validate key_item_id, apply key_consumption_policy_id, and save lock_id before playing the opening sequence.
implementation_notes.unreal: Store lock_id and required key data in an Actor Component, with the server or authority validating inventory before opening replicated doors. Use Gameplay Tags or inventory item ids for keys, and guard against repeated interaction RPCs.
implementation_notes.web: Represent locks as data records keyed by lock_id and inventory entries keyed by item instance. In Phaser or custom JavaScript, keep the confirm handler idempotent so double clicks or retries cannot consume the key twice.

relationships.reason: Key-lock puzzles need readable prompts when a valid key, locked object, or missing requirement is targeted.
Ability locks and key locks can stack into confusing gates unless dependencies are shown clearly.
Procedural key-lock layouts need graph validation so keys appear before required locks.

example_games.title: The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time
example_games.note: Uses keys and locked doors as dungeon progression gates.

example_games.title: Resident Evil 2
example_games.note: Uses themed keys to gate routes and encourage backtracking.
